equipment.description: Large vertical turbine pump used to draw water from rivers, lakes, or oceans into the treatment plant.
Continuously moving mesh screen used to remove large debris, fish, and vegetation from intake water.
Positive displacement pump used to accurately dose coagulants like alum or ferric chloride into the raw water.
High-speed mechanical mixer used to rapidly and evenly disperse coagulant chemicals into the water stream.
Slow-moving paddle or propeller mixer that gently agitates water to encourage the formation of larger floc particles.
Mechanical rake or flight system that slowly pushes settled sludge along the bottom of a clarifier to a collection hopper.
Inclined PVC tubes installed in a sedimentation basin to increase the effective settling area and improve efficiency.
Pump designed to handle thick, viscous sludge, transferring it from clarifiers to thickeners or dewatering equipment.
High-pressure pump that recirculates a portion of clarified water to be supersaturated with air in a DAF system.
Pressure vessel where recycled water is mixed with compressed air to achieve supersaturation for a DAF system.
Large concrete basin containing layers of sand and anthracite used to physically strain remaining suspended solids from the water.
High-capacity pump used to reverse the flow of water through a filter bed to clean the accumulated solids.
Rotary lobe or centrifugal blower that provides compressed air to agitate filter media during the backwash cycle.
Pump that provides the necessary pressure to push water through microfiltration or ultrafiltration membrane modules.
Hollow fiber or spiral wound membrane unit used to remove fine particles, bacteria, and some viruses from the water.
Multi-stage centrifugal or positive displacement pump that generates the high pressure required to overcome osmotic pressure in an RO system.
Fiberglass or stainless steel housing that contains and supports the spiral-wound RO membrane elements.
Device (e.g., isobaric chamber or Pelton wheel) that captures pressure energy from the RO brine stream and transfers it to the feed stream.
Vessel containing replaceable filter cartridges, typically used as pre-filtration before RO membranes.
Equipment that passes oxygen or dry air through a high-voltage electrical field to produce ozone gas for disinfection and oxidation.
Baffled tank where ozone gas is injected and mixed with water to provide sufficient contact time for treatment.
Catalytic or thermal device that converts unreacted, potentially hazardous off-gas ozone back into oxygen.
Vessel containing ultraviolet lamps that irradiate the water to inactivate pathogens without adding chemicals.
Weighing scale used to monitor the remaining amount of liquid chlorine in a storage cylinder.
Safety device mounted on a chlorine cylinder that reduces gas pressure to a vacuum, preventing pressurized leaks.
Venturi device that uses a flow of water to create a vacuum, drawing chlorine gas and mixing it into solution.
Corrosion-resistant tank (e.g., HDPE or FRP) used to store bulk liquid bleach for disinfection.
Pump used to dose ammonia into chlorinated water to form chloramines for a long-lasting residual disinfectant.
Tank where solid sodium fluorosilicate is dissolved in water to create a saturated solution for dosing.
Volumetric or gravimetric feeder used to dispense dry PAC powder into a mixing slurry tank for taste and odor control.
Pressure vessel or gravity basin filled with GAC media to adsorb organic compounds and improve water taste and odor.
Large underground or above-ground reservoir used to store treated water and provide final disinfection contact time.
Large centrifugal pump used to push treated potable water from the clearwell into the municipal distribution network.
Hydropneumatic tank connected to a pumping main to absorb pressure spikes (water hammer) caused by pump starts or valve closures.
Automatic control valve that modulates to maintain a minimum upstream pressure, regardless of downstream demand.
Automatic valve located at the base of an elevated storage tank that opens to fill the tank and closes when full.
Instrument that measures the volumetric flow rate of conductive water using a magnetic field, with no moving parts.
Non-contact sensor that measures water level in tanks or open channels by bouncing sound waves off the surface.
Continuous analyzer that measures the cloudiness (turbidity) of water, crucial for monitoring filter performance.
Continuous analyzer that measures the acidity or alkalinity of the water to control chemical dosing.
Continuous analyzer that measures the concentration of free or total chlorine to ensure adequate disinfection.
Instrument that measures the net electrical charge of particles in water to optimize coagulant dosing.
Picket-fence style rotating mechanism in a circular tank that gently stirs sludge to release trapped water and increase solids concentration.
Skid-mounted system that mixes concentrated liquid or dry polymer with water to create an activated dilute solution for sludge conditioning.
Batch dewatering machine that uses high pressure to squeeze water out of sludge between series of filter cloths.
Continuous dewatering machine that uses tensioned belts to squeeze water out of conditioned sludge.
High-speed rotating machine that uses centrifugal force to separate solid sludge from liquid centrate.
Electronic controller that varies the speed of an electric motor (e.g., on a pump) to match process demand and save energy.
Floor-mounted assembly of cubicles containing motor starters, circuit breakers, and control logic for plant equipment.
Industrial computer that monitors inputs (sensors) and controls outputs (pumps, valves) based on a programmed sequence.

equipment.type: Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) Server
equipment.description: Central computer system that collects data from PLCs, provides a graphical interface for operators, and logs historical trends.
